coredata.dc:title: The potential of nano-enabled oral ecosystem surveillance for respiratory disease management
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Yuhong
Chen, Lin
Zhang, Hao
Nie, Guangjun
Zhang, Zhijian
Zhu, Motao
coredata.dc:description: The association between oral disorders and respiratory diseases has been revealed for decades. Emerging oral biomarkers, including microbiome, metabolites, proteins, and nucleic acids, have been gradually discovered and proposed to contribute to the development and progression of both oral and respiratory diseases. While promising, oral ecosystem surveillance remains confined due to the constant changes in the oral ecosystem and the lack of sophisticated assessment technologies. With the advent of nanotechnology, efficient diagnostics and real-time monitoring of disease are implemented via platforms such as biomarker-capturing nanoparticles, nanobiosensors, nano-microfluidics, and wearable devices. In this review, the association between the oral ecosystem and respiratory system, especially the clinical relevance and cross-regulatory mechanisms, will be first discussed. Saliva, serving as a readily available biofluid for non-invasive diagnostics, will be overviewed for the emerging biomarkers in respiratory disease diagnosis. The latest nanotechnology-enabled approaches will be highlighted in terms of oral biomarker enrichment, multiplexing analysis, sensitive and selective diagnosis, and nanodentistry. Finally, the incorporation of nano-enabled approaches into routine clinical practice will be prospected for future translation.

Biological mechanisms in the association between oral environment and respiratory diseases
Current oral biomarkers for the diagnosis of respiratory diseases
Potential of nanodentistry in the management of respiratory diseases

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Possible mechanisms involved in the cross-regulation between the oral cavity and respiratory diseases. 1) Inhalation of pathogen-containing droplets increases the risk of developing respiratory diseases. 2) Periodontium microorganisms enter the gingival microvasculature and cause hematogenous dissemination. 3) Cytokines and bioactive substances due to periodontal microorganisms facilitate the progression of respiratory inflammation. 4) Bronchial inflammation increases the periodontium bacteria load through expectoration. (Created by BioRender.com).

Infection of oral microbial pathogens could strengthen the pathogenicity of respiratory pathogens. Respiratory epithelium co-infected with respiratory and oral pathogens tends to have an elevated cytokine production leading to the apoptosis of respiratory epithelium cells.

Schematic illustration of a nano-empowered biosensor. The target analytes mixed in human biofluid for detection can be captured by the biorecognition probes immobilized to a surface. The signal generated through the interaction between analytes and probes can be amplified by a transducer and processed into visible data for analysis. (Created in BioRender.com).

(b) Ag nanoparticles modified nitrogen-doped graphene (NFG) are covered by the polyaniline (PANI) nanocomposite and attached to the surface of the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) electrode. head.ce:title: The potential of nano-enabled oral ecosystem surveillance for respiratory disease management

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The association between oral disorders and respiratory diseases has been revealed for decades. Emerging oral biomarkers, including microbiome, metabolites, proteins, and nucleic acids, have been gradually discovered and proposed to contribute to the development and progression of both oral and respiratory diseases. While promising, oral ecosystem surveillance remains confined due to the constant changes in the oral ecosystem and the lack of sophisticated assessment technologies. With the advent of nanotechnology, efficient diagnostics and real-time monitoring of disease are implemented via platforms such as biomarker-capturing nanoparticles, nanobiosensors, nano-microfluidics, and wearable devices. In this review, the association between the oral ecosystem and respiratory system, especially the clinical relevance and cross-regulatory mechanisms, will be first discussed. Saliva, serving as a readily available biofluid for non-invasive diagnostics, will be overviewed for the emerging biomarkers in respiratory disease diagnosis. The latest nanotechnology-enabled approaches will be highlighted in terms of oral biomarker enrichment, multiplexing analysis, sensitive and selective diagnosis, and nanodentistry. Finally, the incorporation of nano-enabled approaches into routine clinical practice will be prospected for future translation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oral cavity, the first bodily entrance linking external and internal environment, is an ecosystem comprising three major parts: oral tissues, saliva, and oral microbiota. Being constantly exposed to a variety of endogenous and exogenous substances (e.g., food, drinks, medications, salivary components, and microorganisms migrating from the nasal cavity, respiratory tract, or gastrointestinal tract), the oral ecosystem is dynamically changing and subjects to many factors. The oral microbiome, being the second-largest microbial community after the gut in humans, harbours a variety of microorganisms that are associated with local dental caries, dental plaque, periodontitis, as well as systemic diseases especially infections [1–3]. Saliva, a habitat for shedding cells and microorganisms that comprising of metabolites, proteins, enzymes, mucins, cytokines, peptides, and electrolytes, is controlled by autonomic nerves and non-dietary factors/stimulus [4]. Persistent exposure to internal or external factors could affect the homeostasis of the oral ecosystem, leading to salivary gland disorder, dental diseases, and even physiological/pathological changes in the system [5–7]. Hence, the status of the oral ecosystem, shaped by the complex local-systemic cross-talks, are potential indicators for both oral and systemic health conditions [8].

Clinical and epidemiological studies have revealed the correlation of oral ecosystem disorder with the incidence of a series of systemic diseases including respiratory diseases, cardiovascular diseases, low birth weight, and diabetes mellitus [8,9]. In particular, respiratory disease, the second leading cause of death worldwide, has been associated with oral ecosystem disorder for decades [10]. Various pathogens within the oral cavity including viruses, bacteria, archaea, fungi, and protozoa have been acknowledged as potential inducements for infectious pulmonary diseases [11]. Many studies have revealed that the incidence and progression of respiratory diseases such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), pneumonia, asthma and lung cancer are intimately related to the disorder of the oral ecosystem, owing to the damaged oral tissues or the abnormality of oral resident microbiota [11–14]. Of note, patients with respiratory diseases are also frequently associated with poor oral health conditions [14,15].

Although the oral-lung cross-regulations were gradually discovered, the early diagnosis or disease monitoring using oral biomarkers remains under establishment. Many lung disease patients remain unrevealed due to the lack of efficient diagnostics. According to the national studies from different regions worldwide, approximately 10–95% of COPD patients are under-diagnosed, while 5–60% of patients are over-diagnosed [16]. Conventional diagnostic approaches for lung diseases like spirometry and imaging lack high sensitivity and specificity. Diagnosis for infectious lung diseases using sputum bacterial culture is time-consuming, and the sampling of sputum is challenging especially in elder and pediatric patients. Other approaches using biological fluid [e.g., blood, bronchoalveolar lavage fluid (BALF)] can be invasive and may increase the risk of getting complications during the diagnostic procedure [17]. Notably, biomarker has emerged as a dynamic and powerful tool to assess early diagnosis, disease progression, and response to disease intervention. Biomarkers such as nitric oxide exhaled, eosinophils from sputum or infectious microorganisms from the blood have been successfully applied to improve the diagnosis of respiratory diseases [18]. Recently, saliva has been proposed as an alternative biofluid for respiratory disease diagnosis by detecting the levels of a wide range of biomarkers including enzymes, proteins, peptides, cytokines and pathogenic microorganisms. Compared to conventional diagnostic biofluids, saliva is more accessible and non-invasive for patient compliance when sampling [19]. Moreover, saliva is more stable compared to blood since it would not clot after sampling [20,21]. Evidently, when a tremendous number of routine detections for COVID-19 are required at a fast speed during the pandemic, saliva has gradually replaced other biofluids in the diagnosis [22]. However, the use of saliva for other respiratory disease diagnosis remains hindered due to the undefined disease-related salivary biomarkers, the low levels of biomarker production presented in saliva is another major limitation. Novel biomarker discovery combined with advanced diagnostic technologies is expected to revolutionize the modalities of respiratory disease diagnosis and monitoring and may ultimately reduce the morbidity and mortality rates.

Nowadays, the emergence of nanotechnology has attracted numerous attention in biomarker detection and disease intervention [23,24]. Harnessing the superior properties of nanomaterials and integrating the advanced nano/micro-fabrication technologies, great promises were held in increasing the disease detection sensitivity, lowering the detection limits, performing multiplexing analysis, and enabling in situ and real-time diagnosis [25]. Nano-enabled approaches for salivary biomarker detection are expected to overcome the costly, time-consuming, instrument-demanding issues from conventional diagnostics such as bacteria culture, immunoassay, proteomics, and real-time polymerase chain reaction (qPCR). For example, by modifying nanoparticles with different ligands or antibodies, engineered nanoparticles can specifically enrich multiple diagnostic biomarkers simultaneously to increase the sensitivity for subsequent analysis [26–28]. The nano-based biosensor can rapidly recognize and respond to specific biomarkers for point-of-care testing [29,30]. It is also inspiring to integrate smart nano-enabled theranostics for in situ real-time disease monitoring and on-demand treatment [31–33]. In this review, we will discuss the interrelationship between the oral ecosystem disorders and respiratory diseases, from the biological mechanisms to the clinical relevance. The current oral biomarkers proposed for the diagnosis of respiratory diseases will be summarized. We will discuss the potential of merging the cutting-edge nanotechnology with conventional diagnostic platforms in biomarker detection and highlight the key studies for oral ecosystem surveillance and intervention for respiratory disease diagnosis and management.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cross-regulation between oral environment and respiratory diseases

ce:section.ce:para: An increasing number of epidemiological and biological studies have been conducted to figure out the relationship between oral ecosystem disorders and the occurrence of respiratory diseases [8,9]. Although no straightforward evidence has proved the causal relationship between oral ecosystem disorders and respiratory diseases, cross-regulation between these two biological systems has been well-documented [34,35]. The pioneer investigation was conducted by Scannapieco et al., who pointed out that individuals with a high oral hygiene index, characterized by more oral debris or/and calculus, are more susceptible to developing chronic respiratory diseases such as COPD [10]. Later, the association between periodontal diseases and COPD has been revealed since individuals with severe attachment loss, probing depth, and gingival index (assessments for periodontal diseases) are at a high risk of COPD and diminished lung function [36,37]. COPD has also been found to give rise to an increased incidence of periodontal diseases in patients [38]. Previous studies have also explored the association between pneumonia and oral health conditions, observing individuals with more dental caries, plaques as well as losing teeth tend to have an elevated risk of developing pneumonia [39,40]. Weekly oral hygiene care can therefore prevent aspiration pneumonia [41]. In recent years, an increasing number of studies have focused on the association between periodontitis and lung cancers. Zeng et al., found that periodontitis was intensively associated with lung cancers, similar to several other meta-analysis studies [42,43].

Apart from the risk of developing respiratory diseases, oral health conditions have also been linked to the exacerbation of existing respiratory diseases, hence timely oral health intervention can help to mitigate the progression of certain respiratory diseases [14,44]. For instance, the exacerbation of COPD is significantly correlated with periodontal and oral hygiene status, assessed by remaining teeth, plaque index, and teeth brushing time [14]. This observation is supported by Kucukcoskun et al., who discovered that improvement in periodontal status is beneficial for reducing COPD exacerbation [45]. Zhou et al., also demonstrated that periodontal treatment could reduce the COPD exacerbation frequency [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological mechanisms in the association between oral environment and respiratory diseases
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the mechanisms that link oral health status to respiratory diseases, plenty of studies have been conducted. As illustrated in Fig. 1, plausible mechanisms accounting for the association involves 1) inhalation of pathogen-containing droplets through the oral-respiratory tract; 2) pathogenic microorganisms colonized in periodontal pocket enter gingival microvasculature and cause hematogenous dissemination; 3) microorganism-stimulated cytokines and bioactive substances diffuse into the circulation and cause inflammation burden; 4) bronchial inflammation facilitates microbial colonization and may, in turn, increase the inflammation/bacteria load in periodontium following expectoration. All of these can either initiate or impact the progression of respiratory diseases and periodontitis.

Owing to the directly connected anatomic structure between respiratory tract and oral cavity, resident pathogens in oral cavity or respiratory tract can migrate between two spaces [46]. Oropharyngeal bacteria could migrate into the lower respiratory tract through respiration, and colonize with pulmonary pathogens to initiate or exacerbate respiratory diseases [47]. For example, the leading factors for periodontitis such as P. gingivalis and A. actinomycetemcomitan are pathogenic bacteria for aspiration pneumonia [48]. Inversely, bacteria that have been associated with community-acquired pneumonia, including S. pneumonia, H. influenza and M. pneumonia, have also been detected in dental plaque [11,49]. The presence of bacterial DNA in lungs indicates that the pulmonary environment is not sterile. The bacteria genera discovered in lungs, including Veillonella, Streptococcus, and Haemophilus, are also dominating pathogens for oral diseases [50,51]. Moreover, these oral pathogens inhaled into the respiratory system could also interact with the respiratory pathogens and further strengthen the pathogenicity of these respiratory pathogens ( Fig. 2) [52]. Conceivably, these pathogens are capable to migrate to the oral cavity via coughing or inhalation and impact the homeostasis of the oral ecosystem.

In addition to the direct translocation of bacteria, the bioactive substances released from oral microorganisms are also involved in the colonization of pathogens in respiratory tract. Salivary pellicle, an acellular biofilm containing a large proportion of mucins, builds a defensive barrier by preventing pathogenic substances from adhesion to the tooth. The mucosal layer of the respiratory tract secreted by the epithelium goblet cells and submucosal gland is also able to prevent pathogens or particles from adhering to the epithelium layer of the respiratory tract. These exogenous substances trapped in this mucosal layer can be eliminated through exhalation or mucus-ciliary escalator, so-called mucosal immunity [53]. However, these biofilms or defensive barriers can be disrupted by excessive hydrolytic enzymes released from oral microorganisms. Elevated levels of hydrolytic enzymes owing to the poor oral hygiene can degrade the mucins of the salivary pellicles and release those wrapped pathogens to the respiratory tract, which increases the risk of getting respiratory diseases [54]. Enzymes like sialidase, hexosaminidase and mannosidase from proteolytic oral bacteria can cause the loss of fibronectin covering the mucosal epithelium, which results in the exposure of adhesion receptors on the mucosal epithelium and increase the attachment of pathogenic microorganisms onto the respiratory epithelium [54].

Cytokines generated from periodontal inflammation can potentially increase the risk of respiratory diseases. Oral pathogens invasion could stimulate epithelial cells, endothelial cells, macrophages and peripheral mononuclear cells of the periodontium to release cytokines [55]. For example, immune cells at the gingival barrier such as dendritic cells and phagocytes could produce pro-inflammatory cytokines [e.g., interleukin (IL)− 6, IL-8, IL-1α, IL-1β and tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α)] upon phagocytosis of pathogenic bacteria. These cytokines enter the respiratory system through inhalation via saliva droplets or diffusion into circulation could stimulate the lung epithelium and recruit inflammatory cells like neutrophils, thus exacerbating the lung inflammation [11,56,57]. Indeed, cytokines including IL-1β, IL-6 and TNF-α were accompanied by periodontal inflammation and elevated in patients’ serum [58,59]. Also, they have been reported to participate in the pathogenesis of COPD and have a negative correlation with lung function [60–62]. Meanwhile, other cytokines like IL-12, IL-4, IL-2 and transforming growth factor (TGF)-β could induce the differentiation of CD4+ T cells and produce a wide range of pro-inflammatory and inflammatory cytokines to cause tissue damage [63]. As for asthma, cytokines including IL-4, IL-13 and interferon (IFN)-γ are responsible for the inflammation aggravation, airway constriction and airway remodeling [64]. In the same vein, cytokines generated due to pulmonary inflammation could also circulate to the periodontal site and aggravate local inflammation [65]. This evidence collectively demonstrated the crosstalk between oral and respiratory systems, and the potential of detecting respiratory diseases by oral environment surveillance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Current oral biomarkers for the diagnosis of respiratory diseases
ce:section.ce:para: To date, oral biomarkers have been applied in the diagnosis of a wide range of diseases from dental to systemic diseases such as diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular diseases, and cancers [66–69]. Although premature, a large number of studies have been conducted, proposing the potential use of oral biomarkers (mainly salivary biomarkers) in the diagnosis of respiratory diseases ( Table 1).

Oral microbiome can be the promising biomarker in the diagnosis of both infectious and non-infectious lung diseases. Taking the latest pandemic as an example, one study has discovered that SARS-CoV-2 can be detected by real-time polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) using saliva samples with a slightly lower sensitivity of 72% compared to the 89% of nasopharyngeal swab [87]. Microbial biomarkers have also been applied in the diagnosis of asthma and COPD. By using 16 S rRNA sequencing, more abundant Streptococcus and Veillonella genera have been discovered in asthmatic patients [77]. As for COPD, the oral microbiota genera Veillonella, Rothia and Actinomyces have been detected more frequently in patients than in healthy individuals, indicating the potential of salivary microbiome for COPD diagnosis [71]. The use of salivary microbiota for lung cancer detection has been investigated as well. Yang et al., have discovered that non-smoking females with lung cancer tend to have an enriched abundance of Sphingomonas and Blastomonas, different from the healthy control groups who harbor abundant Acinetobacter and Streptococcus [85]. In another study, assistant with the detection of Capnocytophaga and Veillonella for squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) diagnosis, the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) for this combined microbial biomarker can reach 0.86 with an 84.6% sensitivity and 86.7% specificity in distinguishing patients from healthy individuals. While for the diagnosis of adenocarcinoma (AC), the ROC value is approximately 0.8 with a 78.6% sensitivity and 80.0% specificity, suggesting the power of oral bacterial biomarkers in assisting the detection and classification of lung cancers [86].

A considerable number of potential protein biomarkers for respiratory diseases have been discovered in saliva. A high level of C-reactive protein (CRP), which is well-recognized as an inflammation marker, has been detected in saliva samples from patients with COPD and pneumonia [44,78,79]. More importantly, the saliva CRP level is strongly correlated with the serum CRP, indicating saliva CRP is a non-invasive alternative to serum, especially for the diagnosis of neonatal and pediatric pneumonia [78,79]. Similarly, an increased level of eosinophil cationic protein (ECP) is detected in asthmatic patients compared with healthy individuals, suggesting to use of saliva ECP as a replacement for serum ECP in the monitoring of the pathogenesis of asthma [76].

While promising, the use of a single salivary biomarker merely realizes the diagnosis of a specific respiratory disease. Hence, more studies are being conducted to investigate the potential of multiple salivary biomarkers in respiratory disease diagnosis. For instance, a combination of saliva CRP, procalcitonin (PCT) and neutrophil elastase (NE) has been applied to achieve point-of-care monitoring and management of COPD exacerbation [70]. Zamora-Mendoza et al., have integrated surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) into the detection of biomarkers (IL-8, IL-10 and sCD163) for asthma, reaching a superior detection sensitivity of 85%, compared with a sensitivity of only 23% from conventional diagnosis [75,88]. While in the diagnosis of lung cancer, three saliva biomarkers [haptoglobin (HP), zinc α2-glycoprotein (AZGP1) and human calprotectin] separated by proteomic and validated by immunoassay, can reach a detection sensitivity of 88.5% and specificity of 92.3%, which is slightly higher than conventional diagnosis approaches but in a more friendly way [84,89].

As for tuberculosis (TB), a combination of granulocyte-colony stimulating factor (G-CSF), TNF-α, and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) can help distinguish TB from other respiratory diseases, yielding a detection sensitivity of 63% and specificity of 63% [72]. In the same study, a combination of serum biomarkers [IL-6, macrophage inflammatory protein-1β (MIP-1β), VEGF] and saliva biomarkers (G-CSF, MIP-1α) can dramatically improve the TB detection sensitivity and specificity to 81.5% and 100%, highlighting the importance of using multiple biomarkers in achieving an ideal detection accuracy [72]. Later, Jacobs et al., managed to use a combination of seven saliva biomarkers [CRP, ferritin, serum amyloid P (SAP), monocyte chemoattractant protein-1 (MCP-1), alpha-2 macroglobulin (A2M), fibrinogen, tissue plasminogen activator (TPA)] to distinguish TB from other respiratory diseases, with a specificity of 83.3%. Moreover, the changes in these saliva biomarkers after 2-month and 6-month of TB treatment can be further analyzed to assess the treatment outcomes [74]. With the attempt to optimize the combination of saliva biomarkers for TB diagnosis, Phalane et al., screened 33 host immunological biomarkers in both saliva and serum samples from individuals suspected of TB disease. Interestingly, marked differences were found in both levels and categories of the molecules detected in saliva and serum: the most abundantly expressed molecules in serum were chemokines [Interferon gamma inducible protein-1 (IP-10), MIP-1β, MCP-1] and acute phase proteins [CRP, serum amyloid A (SAA), SAP], while most abundant molecules in saliva were proinflammatory cytokines and growth factors. A combination of IL-5, IL-6, IL-15, TNF-α, and CRP can achieve a TB detection sensitivity of 81.8% and specificity of 81.4%, which displays a superior accuracy to a combination of other five serum biomarkers in this study [73]. These data suggest the assessment of salivary components may not just become an alternation of blood test, but also reveal undiscovered biological changes beyond the current understanding of the diseases.

The use of saliva nucleic acid biomarkers is predominantly focused on the diagnosis of lung cancer and infectious diseases, and these nucleic acids can be classified into mRNA and saliva cell-free DNA (scfDNA). For saliva mRNA biomarkers, a combination of five mRNA (CCNI, EGFR, FGF19, FRS2 and GREB1) yielded a superior sensitivity of 93.75% and a specificity of 82.81% [83]. CfDNA is degraded DNA fragments that originated from either healthy cells or tumor cells and are released into the circulation. Wei et al., pioneerly applied scfDNA to the diagnosis of lung cancer. By using the electric field-induced release and measurement (EFIRM) platform, the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) mutation can be successfully detected, whose amount is well correlated with the serum EGFR mutation [81]. However, it is still challenging to quantitatively analyze the cfDNA due to its low concentration using the conventional approach [82].

In spite of the promises of oral biomarkers in the detection of respiratory diseases, several key considerations are vital for its application in clinical practice: 1) an optimal selection of multiple parameters is critical for both accuracy and specificity; 2) targeted selection or pre-enrichment is sometimes needed for measuring low-level molecules; 3) the simultaneous detection of multiple biomarkers are required in most cases; 4) a quick turnaround time after sampling is preferred for monitoring the disease progress and treatment outcomes. Hence, novel technologies are being developed to overcome the challenges for oral biomarker detection.

Applying nanotechnology to biomarker detection has attracted growing attention owing to the various utilities of nanomaterials. First, conventional techniques are hard to detect low-level biomarkers present in saliva. Either a highly sensitive method or pre-enrichment step is required for analyzing such biomarkers. Functionalized nanomaterials with unique properties (i.e. optical property, electronic/electrochemical property, magnetic property) are of vital importance in biomarker detection by capturing target analytes, lowering the limit of detection, visualizing and/or amplifying biological signals and improving the detection sensitivity and specificity [90]. Second, current detection approaches are unable to be customized for multiplex detection of selected biomarkers simultaneously, especially using limited volume of saliva samples, which is critical for diagnostic accuracy and specificity. The newly developed micro/nanofluidic platforms that integrate separation, concentration, and detection are capable of detecting multiple biomarkers simultaneously with ultra-low volume of sample. At last, current test procedure may be time-consuming and limited for laboratory use, which is inconvenient for real-time monitoring and point-of-care testing. The nanobiosensors manufactured into portable devices are anticipated to fulfil a lab-on-chip detection in the future. While not adequately developed for oral biomarker detection, nanotechnology and the cutting-edge strategies designed for detecting serum biomarkers or artificial biofluids are likely to be transferred to salivary biomarker detection [91–93]. In this section, the use of nanotechnology in bioseparation, biomarker detection, respiratory disease monitoring, and intervention using nanodentistry will be discussed.

Bioseparation by isolating or enriching the target analytes from biofluids such as blood, urine and saliva is necessary for detecting low-level molecules [94]. Traditional techniques used for bioseparation such as centrifugation, filtration, precipitation or chromatography are somewhat time-consuming, expensive and low-efficient [95]. Nanomaterials that are flexibly modified with various specific ligands are capable of capturing a significant amount of analytes (e.g. proteins, DNA, amino acids, and enzymes) with high efficiency and specificity owing to the large surface-to-volume ratio and unique physicochemical properties such as the supermagnetism of iron oxide nanoparticles [96,97].

Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) have gained great attention in the enrichment of biomarkers since MNPs allow all separation steps to conduct efficiently under an external magnetic field [98,99]. In a previous study, lysozyme was isolated via a non-functionalized magnetite/silica core-shell composite microsphere (Fe3O4 @SiO2) from the binary protein mixture of lysozyme and ovalbumin [100]. When the magnetite/silica microsphere Fe3O4 @SiO2 were modified with polyacrylic acid (PAA) chains, Fe3O4 @silica-g-PAA microspheres significantly increased the binding capacity for lysozyme compared with the nonfunctionalized magnetite/silica microspheres, therefore can be utilized to enrich lysozyme from saliva or blood. In another study, researchers analyzed the expression spectrometry of salivary peptides in oral squamous cell carcinomas (OSCC) patients by MNP-enabled separation followed by matrix-assisted laser-desorption ionization-time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF-MS) [101]. Nanoparticle-based magnetic beads were used for selective enrichment of low-molecular-mass peptides based on the weak cation exchange (WCX) principle. This study identified 50 proteins that were significantly different between the OSCC patients and healthy individuals and found a biomarker histatin-3 that correlates with OSCC progression [101]. The tandem of WCX MNPs separation and MALDI-TOF-MS is a powerful platform to discover novel biomarkers in saliva.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) hold great interest in bioseparation due to their exceptional material properties [102]. CNTs have inner voids that can be filled with different sizes from small molecules to large proteins. In addition, the distinct inner and outer surfaces of CNT scaffolds can be used for immobilization of biomolecules for target separation. CNTs also have thermal, electric, and optical properties for subsequent signal transduction upon the recognition of analytes [103]. Among different categories of CNTs including single-walled CNTs (SWCNTs), double-walled CNTs (DWCNTs) and multi-walled CNTs (MWNTs) [104], MWCNTs were recommended over SWCNTs and DWCNTs due to their low cost and high bacterial affinity [105]. MWCNTs have been used to separate different bacterial cells without the need for conjugating specific ligands or antibodies. The binding capacity of MWCNTs to all the bacterial strains was similar, all of which were 2 × 107 CFU/L, indicating that the strain-specific properties of bacteria have little effect on their absorption efficiency onto MWCNTs [106]. The detailed mechanisms by which bacterial cells are nonselectively absorbed to MWCNTs remain largely unknown, but the hydrophobic interaction between the highly hydrophobic surface of MWCNT and the bacterial surface is being proposed, making the absorbent for oral microbiota also applicable [106].

A biosensor for biomarker detection is typically composed of three units: a biorecognition receptor, a signal generator and transducer, and a signal processor. Based on the different signal generators and transducers, biosensors can basically be classified into optical biosensors, electrochemical biosensors, thermal biosensors, electronic biosensors, and gravimetric biosensors, among which the optical and electrochemical biosensors are considered most promising for clinical use ( Fig. 3). With the integration of various nanomaterials (i.e., nanoparticles, nanotubes, nanowires, nanorods, quantum dots) into biosensors, the detection of biomarkers was improved in terms of its sensitivity, specificity, limit of detection, reproducibility, and efficiency [107,108]. These nanobiosensors have extensively been studied in the detection of proteins, enzymes, nucleic acids, microorganisms and are anticipated to be promising in saliva biomarker detection [109].

Electrochemical nanobiosensor is one of the most prevalent biosensors for biomarker detection due to its improved sensitivity, enhanced specificity, label-free analysis and portability. Carbon-based nanostructures, owing to their superior conductivity and low cost, have been widely used in the fabrication of electrochemical nanobiosensors [110–112]. For instance, the glassy carbon electrode modified with nanostructured chemically reduced graphene oxide (CR-GO) can be applied in the detection of various biomolecules including free bases of DNA, hydrogen peroxide, dopamine, uric acid, and ascorbic acid due to the superior electrochemical activity to conventional glassy carbon electrodes [113–115]. Farzin et al., have developed a L-histidine functionalized reduced graphene oxide which can be attached to a multi-walled carbon nanotube for the detection of prostate-specific antigen (PSA) in saliva. The LOD for PSA can be reduced to 2.8 fg/mL by using this nanobiosensor [116]. By modifying nanobiosensor with different capture antibodies, multiple salivary biomarkers could be detected simultaneously. For instance, a small dimension silicon nanowire (SiNW) can be integrated into a field effect transistor (FET) biosensor for the diagnosis of oral squamous cell carcinoma (OCSS) [117]. Modifying the SiNMs with anti-IL-8 antibody and anti-TNF-α antibody enabled a dual detection of both IL-8 and TNF-α with high specificity. Notably, the LOD of IL-8 and TNF-α was reduced to 100 fg/mL when compared with multiplex immunoassay (LOD of 1–10 pg/mL) and electrochemical approach (LOD of 10 pg/mL) [118,119]. Also, these FETs based on SiNWs could be used to establish a portable sensing platform against salivary cortisol for the monitoring of stress, which could potentially tackle the problems of conventional detection approaches like time-consuming and high complexity [120]. A single-walled nanocarbon covered with multilayers of chitosan, gold nanoparticles, and glucose oxidase could be used for the monitoring of glucose and the LOD was reduced to 0.1 mg/dL [121].

Kumar et al., have fabricated a biosensing platform for the detection of cytokeratin fraction 21–1 (CYFRA-21–1) in saliva by using yttria-doped zirconia-reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite. With improved conductivity and enhanced surface area, this nano-based electrochemical biosensor can significantly improve the detection sensitivity, decrease the LOD to 7.2 pg/mL, and expand the linear range from 0.01 to 50 ng/mL (compared to the linear range from 0.01 to 1 ng/mL of a typical immunoassay) [122]. Nanotechnology-improved detection of salivary biomarkers could also facilitate diagnosis of cancer. With the application of silver nanoparticles in surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) for signal amplification, the detection sensitivity and specificity of salivary sialic acid have been increased to 80% and 93% in a clinical study, offering a fast-screening method for breast cancer [123].

Metallic nanoparticles, especially noble metallic nanomaterials are widely employed in electrochemical nanobiosensor due to their superior conductivity, plasmonic property, and biocompatibility [124,125]. To attain high catalytic activity and stability, a combination of metallic nanoparticles and carbon-based scaffolds such as graphene, graphite materials and fullerene are developed for synergistic effects [126–129]. Azimzadeh et al., have fabricated an electrochemical nanobiosensor by decorating gold nanorods (GNRs) on a graphene oxide (GO) sheet ( Fig. 4a) and using oracet blue (OB) as an electroactive label for the detection of plasma miRNA-155 (a biomarker for breast cancer) [130]. This electrochemical nanobiosensor has exhibited a wide linear response from 2 fM to 8 pM and the LOD was markedly reduced to 0.6 fM (Fig. 4a) [130]. Herein the gold nanoparticles can be replaced by silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) for a reduced cost. By grafting the AgNPs on functionalized graphene and nanostructured polyaniline (PANI) for improved conductivity and stability (Fig. 4b), the detection of HER-2 positive cancer cells was achieved by immobilizing anti-human epidermal growth factor receptor-2 (HER-2) antibody on the electrode [126]. The dynamic detection capacity of this biosensor has been expanded to 10–5 × 106 cells/mL and the LOD was dropped down to 2 cells/mL [126].

Optical nanobiosensors fabricated using metallic nanoparticles, magnetic nanoparticles, quantum dots and carbon-based nanostructures have been employed are being intensively developed for visualized diagnostics, relying on the material properties including localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR), surface-enhanced Raman scattering as well as fluorescence [131–138]. Dadmehr et al., have fabricated a metallic nanoparticle with a gold core and a Fe3O4 shell for the detection of DNA methylation ( Fig. 5a) [132]. With the addition of target methylated complementary single-strand DNA (ssDNA) hybridized with dipyridamole (as a fluorescent probe), the LOD was significantly reduced to ∼3.1 × 10-16 M compared with previous DNA detection biosensors [132]. For the detection of bacteria, AuNPs bound with a probe complementary to the genomic DNA of S. aureus can yield a color change from red to blue in the sample [139]. Determined by the colormetric method, this biosensor is approximately four times more sensitive than the conventional PCR method (Fig. 5b) [139].

Quantum dots (QDs) with prolonged fluorescent lifetime, enhanced fluorescent emission and stability are also favored for optical nanobiosensors development [140]. Zhang et al., have constructed a QD-based optical nanobiosensor for the detection of target DNA with an extremely low concentration through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [141]. This optical nanobiosensor is composed of a reporter probe linked with a fluorophore, a capture probe linked with biotin, and a QD conjugated with streptavidin. When the reporter probe captured the target DNA, the illumination of the QD leads to the fluorescent emission of the fluorophore under excitation, indicating the presence of the target DNA. With an extremely low background signal, this nanobiosensor is suitable for trace-level DNA detection since the LOD was reduced to 4.8 fM, compared with conventional molecular beacons whose LOD is 480 fM [141].

Several other nanobiosensors using thermal transducer, mechanical transducer, or a combination of multiple signal transducers have also been developed and sometimes integrated with other biosensor platforms for synergy. Mechanical transducer and optical transducer have been combined to detect ultralow levels of carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) for early cancer diagnosis [142]. Capture antibodies were anchored to a silicon microcantilever which mechanically resonates due to the mass of the capture and amplified the plasmonic signal as an optical cavity [142]. The secondary antibody linked to AuNPs could increase the capture ability, as well as improve the light absorption and scattering. These dual-functional nanobiosensors reduced the detection limit of CEA and PSA to 0.1 fg/mL and 1 fg/mL, respectively, which is superior to conventional diagnosis approaches, for example, ELISA (∼1–10 pg/mL). Electrochemical and optical nanobiosensor can also be combined for enhanced sensitivity. Fluorescent curcumin immobilized on graphene quantum dots can cover an electrode for a dual-mode of detection of APOe4, a DNA biomarker for Alzheimer’s disease [143]. The electrochemical LOD is 2.18 ng/mL, which is significantly lower than other detection approaches whose LOD mostly exceed 60 ng/mL. The optical mode of this nanobiosensor, while exhibiting a slightly higher LOD of ∼12 ng/mL, can provide complementary information with high sensitivity, reproducibility and repeatability [143].

Microfluidics is a technology which allows fluid manipulation in micrometer channels with an amount as low as a few picolitres. It enables the manufacture of millions of microchannels on a single chip and assessment of biochemical reactions at very small volumes. Microfluidics is also well-known as a micro total analysis system (μTAS) or part of lab-on-a-chip (LOC) platform, which integrates several analytical functions on a miniaturized device [144,145]. Recently, numerous microfluidic devices, such as microfluidic assay, droplet microfluidics, microfluidic paper-based analytical device (μPAD), and slip-driven device, have been extensively applied for biofluid analysis and disease diagnosis [146–149]. The integration of nanotechnology into microfluidic devices further expanded the analytical spectrum, especially for multiplexed analysis of biomarkers in a complex biomixture and improved the detection sensitivity and selectivity [150,151]. Magnetic nanoparticles, quantum dots, Ag nanoparticles, and CNTs are applied in microfluidics by virtue of their extraordinary magnetic or optical properties, specific catalytic properties, and high surface-to-volume ratio, all of which could contribute to the sample separation, signal transduction, fast reactions, and visualization [152].

An increasing number of studies have been carried out to explore nano-based microfluidics in analyzing biomarkers including proteins, nucleic acids, lipids and metabolites [153]. Noble metal nanoparticles, primarily gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), have extensively been used in a microfluidic device for lowering the detection limits and throughput analysis of multiple protein biomarkers in a rapid and sensitive manner. For example, AuNPs functionalized with horseradish peroxidase (HRP) were integrated into a microbead-based microfluidic array. The large surface area of AuNPs allows more HRP binding to the nanosphere, thus improving the immunosensor sensitivity of a-fetoprotein (AFP) as low as 0.2 pg/mL in 10 μL of undiluted calf serum [154]. To simultaneously detect multiple acute myocardial infarction (AMI) biomarkers, antibody-functionalized AuNPs have been employed as capture probes and signal-amplifying probes in a microfluidic paper-based immune device (μPAD). The detection limits were lowered to 0.06 pg/mL, 0.4 pg/mL and 0.3 pg/mL for heart-type fatty acid-binding protein (H-FABP), copeptin, and cardiac troponin I (cTn I), respectively [155]. In another study, antibody-functionalized gold nanorods have been integrated into a microfluidic device for parallel and high-sensitivity quantitative measurement of six cytokines (IL-2, IL-4, IL-6, IL-10, IFN-γ, and TNF-α) in a complex serum [156]. Due to the enhancement of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) by the AuNRs, this microfluidic successfully determined the cytokines at concentrations down to 5–20 pg/mL using merely 1 μL serum sample within 40 min ( Fig. 6a), compared with a typical 2–3 h turnaround time using ELISA.

Similar to protein detections, microfluidic devices have also been employed to improve the sensitivity of nucleic acid detection. For example, Zhang et al., developed a microfluidic device where AuNPs were functionalized with HRP, DNA probe, and streptavidin-conjugated quantum dot as a label. A mere amount of 10 copies/μL HPV genome DNA could be detected through 100 times signal enhancement, compared with an off-chip assay that is only capable to detect over 1000 copies/μL DNA [157]. This microfluidic device can be easily customized for the detection of other nucleic acids, including the DNA biomarkers in saliva or other biospecimens, at a comparatively low level. More recently, Han et al., developed an attomolar level ultrasensitive microfluidic chip by taking advantage of the strong, high-density DNA probe adsorption on the locally assembled graphene oxide (GO) nanomaterial, and poly-L-lysine (PLL) assemble detection for efficient miRNA trapping [158]. The platform presents a wide linear detection regime of 1 aM-10 nM and is able to simultaneously detect five miRNAs (miR-125, miR-126, miR-191, miR-155 and miR-21) within 35 min at an ultralow detection limit of 0.146 aM with no amplification (Fig. 6b). By merely consuming 2 μL of sample each time, this device holds excellent potential in the clinical and remote application for disease diagnosis.

Nanotechnology is driving innovations in the development of non-invasive wearable devices for real-time measurement and continuous health monitoring [159,160]. Up to now, several wearable biosensors including mouthguards, dentures and orthodontic braces have been integrated with nanomaterials to realize an in situ oral health monitoring by detecting the humidity of the oral cavity and specific biomolecules [161–163]. For example, Li et al., have reported the development of a transparent, wearable dental fluorescent mouthguard for high-sensitive visualization and accurate localization of hidden dental lesion sites, by detecting the release of volatile sulfur compounds (VSCs) [164]. This fluorescent mouthguard was fabricated with the zinc oxide-poly(dimethylsiloxane) (ZnO-PDMS) nanocomposite material, which is composed of fluorescent zinc oxide quantum dots (ZnO QDs) and fluorescent poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS). On the wearable ZnO-PDMS mouthguard, the fluorescence at specific lesion sites could be selectively quenched by the locally released VSCs of high sensitivity ( Fig. 7). When further applied to screen the dental lesion sited in volunteers, the mouthguards successfully uncovered the precise locations of dental caries through a 7 h monitoring, allowing convenient screening of hidden dental lesion sites that are omitted by dentists [164]. In addition, to satisfy the needs of people living in rural and suburban areas that lack oral health care, Ma et al., reported the development of an easy-to-use, low-cost, and visualized detection platform to screen dental caries and periodontal diseases [165]. Au@Ag nanorods-poly(dimethylsiloxane) (Au@ag NRs-PDMS) wearable mouthguard exhibits a distinct colour response to VSCs generated by bacterial decay at the lesion sites. Due to the excellent chemical stability, good biocompatibility, and low cost, the mouthguard has the potential to be utilized on a large-scale population to help self-monitoring the oral health in daily life [165].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential of nanodentistry in the management of respiratory diseases
ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials or nanodevices have been applied to prevent dental caries and their recurrence, treat dentin hypersensitivity, enhance the properties of root canal sealers, and maintain oral health [166]. Till now, tens of nanoproducts have been approved by FDA for dentistry according to the 510(k) pathway [167]. Applying nanotechnology in dentistry is potentially beneficial in preventing the incidence of respiratory diseases or assisting the intervention of respiratory diseases due to the oral-lung cross-regulation.

As mentioned in previous sections, the accumulation of oral pathogens contributes to dental caries, plaques, and local inflammation, and may therefore serve as potential risk factors for the occurrence of respiratory diseases. Nanomaterials with superior anti-microbial properties, such as AgNPs applied in dental implants or fillings, may therefore contribute to the reduced incidence of respiratory infections. To prevent dental plaque or the formation of biofilms in the oral cavity, AgNPs with antibacterial properties were used as dentine coating materials to inhibit the growth of Streptococcus mutans as a replacement for the conventional disinfectant chlorohexidine [168]. Elder people wearing dentures are highly likely to have microbiome adhering to the denture base leading to infections. Integrating AgNPs into conventional dental materials such as acrylic resin can significantly inhibit the growth of oral microbial pathogens including Streptococcus, Staphylococcus and Candida to prevent the infection [169–171]. Other nanoparticles such as TiO2 nanoparticles, Fe2O3 nanoparticles, and ceramics nanoparticles have been demonstrated effective prophylaxis against microbial colonization [172,173]. Using cationic fluorescein-labelled food-grade starch to fabricate the high biocompatible and biodegradable nanoparticles, the detection of dental caries can be monitored. By specifically targeting the active carious lesions, these nanoparticles can illuminate under a dental composite curing lamp for the early detection of dental caries [174].

Nanotechnology in periodontal disease treatment primarily acts as drug carriers to reduce adverse effects and enhance drug delivery efficiency by depositing the therapeutic agents into a targeted site in a controlled manner [175–178]. Aminu et al., have developed a chitosan-based nanogel loaded with triclosan-contained polycaprolactone nanoparticles and flurbiprofen to execute an antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory dual-function in periodontitis treatment [177]. Encapsulating triclosan into nanoparticles could dramatically optimize its solubility and enhance the penetration into periodontal tissues. Liposome, being another universally used nano-drug carrier, has also been explored in periodontal disease treatment. In addition to the delivery of commonly used antimicrobial agents, liposomes loaded with reporter DNA plasmids have newly been developed as a gene delivery system targeting periodontal tissues for durable gene expression at the gingiva site [179].

In this review, the promises of oral ecosystem surveillance for the early diagnosis and monitoring of respiratory diseases have been implied by the non-invasive sampling procedure, abundant biomarkers, and well-correlation with the conventional tests. While showing great potential, the assessments of oral biomarkers in either clinical practice or point-of-care services are likely to engage cutting-edge technologies for successful implementations. The applications of nanotechnology in both salivary biomarker detection and oral health maintenance are undemanding support for the management of respiratory diseases including prevention, diagnosis, and treatment. Nano-enabled methodologies are expected to 1) detect low levels of biomarkers present in saliva; 2) perform parallel detection of multiple biomarkers; 3) balance between high sensitivity and specificity; and 4) be fast and cost-effective.

However, general biomarkers present in saliva may lack specificity when applied solely in the diagnosis of respiratory diseases. For instance, salivary CRP, as a common inflammatory marker, also indicates the potential of having cardiovascular diseases [180]. Inflammatory cytokines such as salivary IL-8 have been discovered to elevate in patients with tumors or inflammatory bowel disease [181]. In order to increase the diagnostic specificity for respiratory diseases, several approaches using salivary biomarkers have been investigated. One successful approach is to use a combination of selected salivary biomarkers that screened out from a large number of host salivary proteins to improve the specificity [73,74]. In contrast to the protein biomarkers, the microbiome and genome information discovered in saliva are more specific in indicating the microorganism infections in respiratory systems [22,71]. Of note, the main purpose of using saliva specimens in respiratory disease diagnosis is to substitute the invasive sampling of biofluids such as serum and BALF, and to conduct point-of-care testing in a timely fashion. The ultimate diagnosis of respiratory diseases will not be merely based on the oral biomarkers (similar to serum biomarkers) since other diagnostic modalities such as X-ray imaging are still necessary for an accurate diagnosis.

While promising, several issues still need to be emphasized to smooth the future application of nano-facilitated oral ecosystem surveillance in disease diagnosis and intervention. First, standard operating procedures (SOPs) should be established regarding the sampling and storage of saliva to maintain the sample stability and result reproducibility. In terms of portable, user-friendly point-of-care nanodevices or wearable devices, the nanosystem instructions and SOPs are also critical for the device resilience for reliable remote care. Second, with the discovery of an increasing number of novel salivary biomarkers, both research and translational studies should be conducted on larger cohorts for clinical validation before identified as biomarkers. Third, an optimal selection and combination of multiple salivary biomarkers are critical to fulfill a highly specific and sensitive diagnosis of certain respiratory disease, where the artificial intelligence-assisted big-data analysis may be required. By gathering all the necessary information, nanosensors or nano-microfluidics could be customized and tailored designed for the detection of specific collections of biomarkers for efficient disease diagnosis.

Guangjun Nie: Writing – review & editing. Zhijian Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Motao Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.
